questions.question: What topic section is listed under the EAU Guidelines on Prostate Cancer as one of the main navigation sections?

options.text: Epidemiology and Aetiology
Surgical Techniques
Chemotherapy Protocols
Radiation Dosimetry

questions.explanation: The navigation text explicitly lists 'Epidemiology And Aetiology' as one of the main section headings in the EAU Guidelines on Prostate Cancer structure. The other options are plausible medical topics but are not mentioned in the provided text.
questions.source_text: EAU Guidelines on Prostate Cancer - EPIDEMIOLOGY AND AETIOLOGY - Uroweb Science & Publications About Become member Guidelines Prostate Cancer Want to read the guideline in your own time? Download the PDF Download full guideline Looking for a quick overview? Check the pocket guidelines. Download pocket guidelines Searching... Introduction Methods Epidemiology And Aetiology Classification And Staging Systems Diagnostic Evaluation Treatment Follow Up Quality Of Life Outcomes In Prostate Cancer

questions.question: According to the autopsy studies cited, what is the reported prevalence of prostate cancer in men aged 71-80 years in Japan?
questions.explanation: The text states that autopsy-detected prostate cancer prevalence varies among men with different ethnical backgrounds and geographical areas, specifically noting 83% in White US males vs. 41% in Japan at age 71-80.
questions.section_title: 3.2. Aetiology and risk factors for prostate cancer
questions.source_text: Prostate cancer is the second-most diagnosed cancer in men, with an estimated 1.46 million diagnoses and 396,792 deaths worldwide in 2022 [3,4]. In more than half of the countries of the world, PCa is the most frequently diagnosed cancer in men and is the leading cause of death among men in a quarter of all countries [5]. In Europe, PCa is the most frequently diagnosed cancer in men and the third most common cancer-related cause of death in men [6]. An SR of autopsy studies reported a prevalence

questions.question: In which geographical region is the age-standardised incidence rate of prostate cancer diagnosis reported to be the highest?

options.text: Northern America
Northern Europe
Australia/New Zealand
Western Europe

questions.explanation: The text reports that prostate cancer diagnosis incidence is highest in Australia/New Zealand and Northern America, with ASRs per 100,000 of 111.6 and 97.2, respectively, followed by Western and Northern Europe.
questions.section_title: 3.2. Aetiology and risk factors for prostate cancer
questions.source_text: Prostate cancer is the second-most diagnosed cancer in men, with an estimated 1.46 million diagnoses and 396,792 deaths worldwide in 2022 [3,4]. questions.question: According to the guidelines, what is the approximate risk of prostate cancer in first-generation Asian immigrants in the USA compared to their US-born Asian-descendant compatriots?

options.text: Approximately half the risk
Approximately double the risk
Approximately equal risk
Approximately one-quarter of the risk

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that first-generation Asian immigrants in the USA have approximately half the risk of prostate cancer compared to their US-born Asian-descendant compatriots, suggesting a role for environmental and/or dietary factors.
questions.source_text: A wide variety of endogenous and exogenous/environmental factors have been discussed as being associated with the risk of developing PCa, or as being aetiologically important for the progression from latent to clinical PCa [13]. As discussed previously, there is likely a racial factor involved, but first-generation Asian immigrants in the USA have approximately half the risk of PCa when compared to their US-born Asian-descendant compatriots, implying a role for environmental and/or dietary facto

questions.question: According to these guidelines, into which two categories are the risk factors for prostate cancer divided?

options.text: Hereditary and nonhereditary
Genetic and environmental
Endogenous and exogenous
Modifiable and non-modifiable

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that these guidelines divide risk factors into hereditary (including ethnicity, family history, and known genetic mutations) and nonhereditary factors (including dietary and medical factors, metabolic syndrome, and obesity).
questions.source_text: A wide variety of endogenous and exogenous/environmental factors have been discussed as being associated with the risk of developing PCa, or as being aetiologically important for the progression from latent to clinical PCa [13]. As discussed previously, there is likely a racial factor involved, but first-generation Asian immigrants in the USA have approximately half the risk of PCa when compared to their US-born Asian-descendant compatriots, implying a role for environmental and/or dietary facto

questions.question: Which of the following defines hereditary prostate cancer (HPCa)?

options.text: 2 first-degree relatives diagnosed at any age
At least 3 cases in the same family, prostate cancer in three successive generations, or at least 2 cases diagnosed under 55 years
1 first-degree relative and 2 second-degree relatives diagnosed at any age
Any family history of prostate cancer with PSA elevation

questions.explanation: The text explicitly defines hereditary PCa as >=3 cases in the same family, PCa in three successive generations, or >=2 cases in the same family diagnosed <55 years.
questions.source_text: Ethnic background and family history are both associated with varying PCa incidence, suggesting a genetic predisposition [5]. Men of African ancestry in the Western world demonstrate more unfavourable outcomes, that may be due to biological, environmental, social and/or health-care factors [15]. These men have been reported to be at increased risk of being diagnosed with more advanced disease [16] and more likely to be upgraded after prostatectomy than White men [17], but the question is more in

questions.question: According to the text, what is the difference in survival between White, Black or Hispanic men with metastatic prostate cancer?

options.text: Black men have significantly worse overall survival
Hispanic men have better prostate cancer-specific mortality
White men have significantly better outcomes in both measures
No difference in overall survival or prostate cancer-specific mortality

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states there is data suggesting no difference in overall survival (OS) or prostate cancer-specific mortality (PCSM) between White, Black or Hispanic men with metastatic PCa.
questions.source_text: Ethnic background and family history are both associated with varying PCa incidence, suggesting a genetic predisposition [5]. questions.question: According to the guideline text, what percentage of men with metastatic prostate cancer were found to harbor germline mutations in 16 genes including BRCA2, CHEK2, ATM, and BRCA1?

options.text: Up to 5%
Up to 12%
Up to 17%
Up to 20%

questions.explanation: The text states that 'up to 12% of men with metastatic PCa harbour germline mutations in 16 genes (including BRCA2 [5.3%])'.
questions.source_text: Pathogenic germline mutations in the BRCA2 and HOXB13 genes, but also in the genes CHEK2 , BRCA1 , ATM , NBS1 , and genes involved in Lynch syndrome, have been suggested to increase the risk of PCa [5]. Data from the United Kingdom on over 21,000 men without a PCa diagnosis suggest that 1.6% carry a pathogenic mutation in at least one of the genes BRCA2 , HOXB13 or CHEK2 . Although germline mutations leading to PCa are relatively rare (1/300), the impact on PCa risk is quite strong, and the prev

questions.question: What is the relative risk of prostate cancer for men with germline BRCA2 mutations who are diagnosed at 55 years or under?

options.text: RR 2.5 to 4.6
RR 5 to 7
RR 8 to 23
RR 25 to 30

questions.explanation: The text specifies that for men with BRCA2 mutations diagnosed at 55 years or under, the relative risk is RR 8-23, which represents a substantially higher risk compared to the general RR of 2.5 to 4.6.
questions.source_text: Pathogenic germline mutations in the BRCA2 and HOXB13 genes, but also in the genes CHEK2 , BRCA1 , ATM , NBS1 , and genes involved in Lynch syndrome, have been suggested to increase the risk of PCa [5]. Data from the United Kingdom on over 21,000 men without a PCa diagnosis suggest that 1.6% carry a pathogenic mutation in at least one of the genes BRCA2 , HOXB13 or CHEK2 . Although germline mutations leading to PCa are relatively rare (1/300), the impact on PCa risk is quite strong, and the prev

questions.question: According to the text, what is the current status of polygenic risk scores (PRSs) in relation to PSA for risk stratification of prostate cancer?

options.text: PRSs have additive prognostic value when added to PSA and can be used for risk stratification
PRSs have no additive prognostic value when added to PSA and therefore cannot be used for risk stratification
PRSs replace PSA as the primary tool for risk stratification of prostate cancer
PRSs are only used for diagnosis but not for risk stratification of prostate cancer

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'there appears to be no additive prognostic value in the PRSs when added to PSA and PRSs therefore cannot be used for risk stratification.'
questions.source_text: If germline genetic mutations are relatively rare, but with significant impact on PCa risk, SNPs are very common, but each SNP has low impact on the risk of developing PCa [5]. Two hundred and sixty-nine individual SNPs have been identified to be associated with PCa risk [50]. Although each individual SNP has a low impact on PCa risk, the additive effects of multiple alleles can cause substantial increased risk of developing PCa and are likely causative of a large proportion of hereditary PCa [5

questions.question: How do SNPs and germline genetic mutations differ in their characteristics regarding prostate cancer risk?

options.text: SNPs are rare with high individual impact, while germline mutations are common with low individual impact
Both SNPs and germline mutations are equally common and have equal impact on PCa risk
SNPs are very common but each has low individual impact on PCa risk, while germline mutations are relatively rare but have significant impact on PCa risk
SNPs and germline mutations are both very rare and have minimal impact on PCa risk

questions.explanation: The text clearly distinguishes that germline mutations are relatively rare but have significant impact on PCa risk, whereas SNPs are very common but each SNP has low impact on PCa risk.
questions.source_text: If germline genetic mutations are relatively rare, but with significant impact on PCa risk, SNPs are very common, but each SNP has low impact on the risk of developing PCa [5]. Two hundred and sixty-nine individual SNPs have been identified to be associated with PCa risk [50]. questions.question: According to the guideline, what is the BRCA2 germline alteration an independent predictor of?

options.text: PSA screening efficacy
Metastases and worse PCa-specific survival
Response to chemotherapy
Radiotherapy complications

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'BRCA2 germline alteration is an independent predictor of metastases and worse PCa-specific survival [35,42]'.
questions.source_text: There are three basic inherited risk factors that are consistently associated with PCa: ethnicity/family history, rare germline mutations in various candidate genes and common genetic single-nucleotide polymorphism (SNPs). 3.2.1.a. Ethnicity and Family history Ethnic background and family history are both associated with varying PCa incidence, suggesting a genetic predisposition [5]. Men of African ancestry in the Western world demonstrate more unfavourable outcomes, that may be due to biologica

questions.question: Which definition correctly describes familial PCa according to the guideline?

options.text: ≥3 cases in the same family diagnosed before 55 years
2 first-degree relatives diagnosed with PCa at any age
PCa in three successive generations
1 first-degree relative with ≥2 second-degree relatives diagnosed before 60 years

questions.explanation: The guideline Table 1 defines familial PCa as '2 first-degree relatives diagnosed with PCa at any age or 1 first-degree relative and ≥2 second-degree relatives diagnosed at any age.' Option A and C describe hereditary PCa, not familial PCa.
questions.source_text: There are three basic inherited risk factors that are consistently associated with PCa: ethnicity/family history, rare germline mutations in various candidate genes and common genetic single-nucleotide polymorphism (SNPs). 3.2.1.a. questions.question: According to the REDUCE study, what was the association between obesity and prostate cancer risk?

options.text: Obesity was associated with higher risk of both low-grade and high-grade PCa
Obesity was associated with lower risk of low-grade PCa and higher risk of high-grade PCa
Obesity was associated with higher risk of low-grade PCa and lower risk of high-grade PCa
Obesity was not associated with any significant change in PCa risk

questions.explanation: The text states that within the REDUCE study, obesity was associated with lower risk of low-grade PCa (OR: 0.79, p=0.01) and a higher risk of high-grade PCa (OR: 1.28, p=0.042) in multivariable analyses.
questions.source_text: The association between metabolic syndrome and PCa is not clear, with mixed results in various studies. There appears to be a weak association overall, but a slightly stronger association in the subgroup of men with more aggressive disease [5]. The single components of metabolic syndrome that have been the most strongly associated with a significantly greater risk of PCa are hypertension (p = 0.035) and waist circumference ≥ 102 cm (p = 0.007) [53]. A SR found a sli

questions.question: What did the meta-analysis of fourteen large prospective studies conclude regarding blood cholesterol levels and PCa risk?

options.text: High total cholesterol was associated with increased PCa risk
Low HDL cholesterol was associated with reduced PCa risk
No association was found between blood cholesterol levels and PCa risk
Elevated LDL cholesterol was associated with higher-grade PCa

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that a meta-analysis of fourteen large prospective studies did not show any association between blood total cholesterol, HDL cholesterol, LDL cholesterol levels, and the risk of developing either overall PCa or high-grade PCa.
questions.source_text: The association between metabolic syndrome and PCa is not clear, with mixed results in various studies. There appears to be a weak association overall, but a slightly stronger association in the subgroup of men with more aggressive disease [5]. The single components of metabolic syndrome that have been the most strongly associated with a significantly greater risk of PCa are hypertension (p = 0.035) and waist circumference ≥ 102 cm (p = 0.007) [53]. questions.question: According to the guideline text, what is the association between coffee consumption and prostate cancer risk?

options.text: Coffee consumption is definitely protective against PCa based on RCTs
Coffee consumption may be associated with a reduced risk of PCa with a pooled RR of 0.91
Coffee consumption has no effect on PCa risk
Coffee consumption increases the risk of PCa

questions.explanation: The text states that 'Coffee consumption may be associated with a reduced risk of PCa, with a pooled RR of 0.91 for the highest category of coffee consumption.' The word 'may' indicates this is a potential association, not a definitive one, and the RR of 0.91 suggests a slight protective effect.
questions.source_text: The association between a wide variety of dietary factors and PCa have been studied, but there is a paucity of quality evidence (Table 3.3). To date, the current body of evidence will not support a causal relationship between specific (dietary and otherwise) factors and the development of PCa. Consequently, no effective preventative strategies can be suggested. Table 3.3: Main dietary factors that have been associated with PCa Alcohol High alcohol intake, but also total abstention from alcohol h

questions.question: According to the guideline text, what is the relationship between alcohol intake and prostate cancer risk?

options.text: Only high alcohol intake is associated with higher PCa risk
Only total abstention from alcohol is associated with higher PCa risk
Both high alcohol intake and total abstention are associated with higher PCa risk
Alcohol intake has no association with PCa risk

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'High alcohol intake, but also total abstention from alcohol has been associated with a higher risk of PCa and PCa-specific mortality.' This indicates a U-shaped relationship where both extremes of alcohol consumption are linked to increased PCa risk.
questions.source_text: The association between a wide variety of dietary factors and PCa have been studied, but there is a paucity of quality evidence (Table 3.3). To date, the current body of evidence will not support a causal relationship between specific (dietary and otherwise) factors and the development of PCa. Consequently, no effective preventative strategies can be suggested. Table 3.3: Main dietary factors that have been associated with PCa Alcohol High alcohol intake, but also total abstention from alcohol h

questions.question: According to the EAU guidelines, the potential benefit of 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors (5-ARIs) in preventing prostate cancer applies to which grade group?

options.text: All prostate cancer grade groups
Only ISUP Grade Group 1 cancer
High-grade prostate cancers only
Prostate cancers requiring radical treatment

questions.explanation: The guideline states that 5-ARIs decrease prostate cancer risk by 25% but only for ISUP GG 1 cancer. This effect is limited to low-grade tumors.
questions.source_text: 3.2.2.c.1. 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors (5-ARIs) Although it appears that 5-ARIs have the potential of preventing or delaying the development of PCa (decreasing the risk by 25% but only for ISUP GG 1 cancer), this must be weighed against treatment-related side effects as well as the potential small increased risk of high-grade PCas (although this does not appear to impact PCa mortality) [88-90]. None of the available 5-ARIs have been approved by the European Medicines Agency (EMA) for chemopreve

questions.question: What is the effect of testosterone supplementation on prostate cancer risk in hypogonadal men according to the EAU guidelines?

options.text: Increases the risk of developing prostate cancer
Has no effect on prostate cancer risk
Does not increase the risk of developing prostate cancer
Decreases prostate cancer-specific mortality only

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that hypogonadal men receiving testosterone supplements do not have an increased risk of developing prostate cancer.
questions.source_text: 3.2.2.c.1. 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors (5-ARIs) Although it appears that 5-ARIs have the potential of preventing or delaying the development of PCa (decreasing the risk by 25% but only for ISUP GG 1 cancer), this must be weighed against treatment-related side effects as well as the potential small increased risk of high-grade PCas (although this does not appear to impact PCa mortality) [88-90]. None of the available 5-ARIs have been approved by the European Medicines Agency (EMA) for chemopreve

questions.question: According to the guideline, which of the following lifestyle factors is associated with a DECREASED risk of prostate cancer (PCa)?

options.text: Current cigarette smoking, which is associated with increased PCa death (RR: 1.24)
Higher ejaculation frequency (≥21 times/month vs. 4-7 times), associated with 20% lower PCa risk
Occupational exposure to chemicals and pesticides, which increases PCa risk
Night-shift work, associated with increased PCa risk in a dose-dependent manner

questions.explanation: The text states that higher ejaculation frequency (≥21 times/month vs. 4-7 times) is associated with a 20% lower risk of PCa. In contrast, cigarette smoking, occupational chemical/pesticide exposure, and night-shift work are all associated with INCREASED PCa risk.
questions.source_text: Taller height, potentially due to higher levels of insulin-like growth factor during puberty, and vertex pattern baldness, has been reported to be associated with an increased risk of PCa [5,94]. A significantly higher rate of ISUP GG ≥ 2 PCa (hazard ratio [HR]: 4.04) was found in men with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) when compared with the general population [95]. However, in an SR, the results on IBD overall were mixed, except for the subgroup of ulcerative colitis, where a clear

questions.question: Which of the following factors has been DISPROVEN as a risk factor for prostate cancer?

options.text: Human papillomavirus type 16 infection, which may increase PCa risk
Vasectomy, which was previously linked but has now been disproven as a risk factor
Gonorrhoea, significantly associated with increased PCa incidence (OR: 1.31)
Taller height due to higher insulin-like growth factor levels during puberty

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that vasectomy 'has been disproved' as a risk factor for PCa. HPV type 16 may increase risk, gonorrhoea has a significant association (OR: 1.31), and taller height is listed as associated with increased risk.
questions.source_text: Taller height, potentially due to higher levels of insulin-like growth factor during puberty, and vertex pattern baldness, has been reported to be associated with an increased risk of PCa [5,94]. A significantly higher rate of ISUP GG ≥ 2 PCa (hazard ratio [HR]: 4.04) was found in men with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) when compared with the general population [95]. questions.question: According to the REDUCE study, what is the association between obesity and prostate cancer risk?

options.text: Obesity is associated with higher risk of both low-grade and high-grade PCa
Obesity is associated with lower risk of low-grade PCa and higher risk of high-grade PCa
Obesity is associated with lower risk of high-grade PCa and higher risk of low-grade PCa
Obesity has no significant association with PCa risk

questions.explanation: The text states that in the REDUCE study, obesity was associated with lower risk of low-grade PCa (OR: 0.79, p=0.01) and a higher risk of high-grade PCa (OR: 1.28, p=0.042) in multivariable analyses.
questions.section_title: 3.2.3. Summary of evidence for epidemiology and aetiology
questions.source_text: There are a number of risk factors for PCa, which are less determined by ethnicity and/or heredity, of which age is the most obvious [7]. 3.2.2.a. Metabolic syndrome The association between metabolic syndrome and PCa is not clear, with mixed results in various studies. There appears to be a weak association overall, but a slightly stronger association in the subgroup of men with more aggressive disease [5]. questions.question: Regarding lycopene and prostate cancer prevention, what is the current evidence from randomized controlled trials (RCTs)?

options.text: RCTs show a significant decrease in PCa incidence with lycopene supplementation
RCTs show lycopene increases PCa risk
RCTs comparing lycopene with placebo did not identify a significant decrease in PCa incidence
No RCTs have been conducted on lycopene and PCa

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that RCTs comparing lycopene with placebo did not identify a significant decrease in the incidence of PCa, despite meta-analyses showing a trend towards favorable effect of tomato intake and lycopenes.
